step2_rag_chunks: Avoid situations where others are smoking during the early stages of quitting. Being around smokers can increase the urge to smoke.

step2_rag_chunks: Exploring why a patient wants to quit â€” whether for health, family, finances, or personal reasons â€” helps build intrinsic motivation that sustains the quit attempt during difficult moments. The stronger and more personal the reason, the more effective it is as a motivational anchor.

There are two kinds of triggers â€” situational and emotional. Situational triggers are external: after meals, with tea, during a work break. Emotional triggers are internal: stress, boredom, loneliness, anxiety. Most people have both. Knowing which type is stronger for you helps us choose the right approach.
Making a daily plan for managing cravings can make the quitting process easier.

step2_rag_chunks: Replacing smoking with another activity such as chewing gum, eating healthy snacks, or walking can help break the habit.

After meals, immediately introduce a competing behavior before the urge has a chance to form â€” get up from the table, brush your teeth, chew sugar-free gum, or take a short walk. The goal is to break the automatic link between finishing a meal and reaching for a cigarette by making a different behavior equally automatic over time.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

step2_rag_chunks: Cravings for tobacco are temporary and usually last between 5 and 10 minutes. When a craving occurs, delay smoking and focus on another activity until the urge passes.
The 4D technique helps manage cravings: Delay smoking for a few minutes, take Deep breaths slowly, Drink water, and Do something else to distract yourself.

When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

Therapist: It sounds like you're feeling a bit stuck, still thinking about the link between driving, stress, and smoking. Can you imagine how it might feel to have a moment of calm while driving, just by listening to an audiobook or making a call?

Patient: Yeah, it's possible, I guess. I mean, I've had moments like that in the past, but they didn't last long. I was trying that once before, to reduce my smoking to 10 a day, but I found myself craving a cigarette during the most stressful parts of my route. It was hard to keep it up.
Therapist: It sounds like you've tried reducing your smoking to 10 a day before, but it didn't work out because of the stress you experience during your route.
What do you think is the most stressful part of your route that led to cravings for a cigarette?

Patient: It's usually the traffic, especially when I'm stuck in a jam. It's frustrating and I feel like I'm wasting time, which makes me anxious. Then there's the long hours alone, it can get monotonous and I find myself reaching for a cigarette just to break the monotony, I guess.

Therapist: You're saying that traffic jams and long hours alone are the most stressful parts of your route that make you crave a cigarette. How do you think you could manage your frustration while stuck in traffic, maybe find a way to look at it differently?

Patient: I don't know, it's hard to see it as anything but frustrating. But I guess if I could think of it as a chance to listen to something or catch up on some work, it might make the time go by faster. Or I could try to use that time to relax, breathe, or something. But that's hard to do when I'm sitting still in traffic.
